message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To limit societal and economic costs of lockdown measures, public health strategies are needed that control the spread of SARS-CoV-2 and simultaneously allow lifting of disruptive measures. Regular universal random screening of large proportions of the population regardless of symptoms has been proposed as a possible control strategy.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We developed a mathematical model that includes test sensitivity depending on infectiousness for PCR-based and antigen-based tests, and different levels of onward transmission for testing and non-testing parts of the population. Only testing individuals participate in high-risk transmission events, allowing more transmission in case of unnoticed infection. We calculated the required testing interval and coverage to bring the effective reproduction number due to universal random testing (
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>rt</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    ) below 1, for different scenarios of risk behavior of testing and non-testing individuals.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    With
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    = 2.5, lifting all control measures for tested subjects with negative test results would require 100% of the population being tested every three days with a rapid test method with similar sensitivity as PCR-based tests. With remaining measures in place reflecting
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>e</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    = 1.3, 80% of the population would need to be tested once a week to bring
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>rt</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    below 1. With lower proportions tested and with lower test sensitivity, testing frequency should increase further to bring
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>rt</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    below 1. With similar
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>e</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    values for tested and non-tested subjects, and with tested subjects not allowed to engage in higher risk events, at least 80% of the populations needs to test every five days to bring
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>rt</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    below. The impact of the test-sensitivity on the reproduction number is far less than the frequency of testing.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Interpretation</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Regular universal random screening followed by isolation of infectious individuals is not a viable strategy to reopen society after controlling a pandemic wave of SARS-CoV-2. More targeted screening approaches are needed to better use rapid testing such that it can effectively complement other control measures.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Funding</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>RECOVER (H2020-101003589) (MJMB), ZonMw project 10430022010001 (MK, HH), FCT project 131_596787873 (GR). ZonMw project 91216062 (MK)</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Regular universal screening for SARS-CoV-2 infection may not allow reopening of society after controlling a pandemic wave
